does: This paper introduces BRT-Align, a control-theoretic framework that treats LLM generation as a dynamical system evolving in latent space and applies backward reachability analysis to learn a safety value function. The method enables two capabilities at inference time: a runtime monitor that predicts unsafe completions several tokens before they occur, and a steering filter that makes minimal perturbations to latent states to redirect generation away from unsafe regions. The authors demonstrate across multiple LLMs and toxicity benchmarks that BRT-Align achieves more accurate and earlier detection of unsafe continuations compared to baseline approaches, addressing the critical limitation that RLHF provides no safeguards during inference when harmful outputs can still be generated.
connects: This work bridges control theory and LLM safety, extending recent efforts to apply dynamical systems perspectives to neural network behavior. It builds on the foundational insight that RLHF, while effective at training time, leaves models vulnerable to unsafe generation at inference, motivating the need for runtime safety mechanisms. The paper connects to prior work on value functions for trajectory prediction in reinforcement learning and reachability analysis in formal verification and control systems. It also relates to concurrent research on steering language models through intervention in activation space and detecting model misbehavior through latent dynamics, combining these threads with the formal guarantees that backward reachability provides about worst-case behavior.
enables: This framework opens several research directions for verified safety in generative models. It enables more rigorous, computationally tractable approaches to certifying model behavior by casting safety as a reachability problem amenable to formal analysis. The work points toward hybrid safety systems that combine training-time alignment with inference-time guarantees, potentially enabling deployment of LLMs in high-stakes domains where current methods lack sufficient assurance. Follow-up work could extend the approach to multi-step lookahead, incorporate user preferences into steering objectives, scale to larger models, and explore whether reachability analysis provides theoretical bounds on safety violations. This could inspire broader adoption of control-theoretic tools in AI safety research beyond toxicity detection.
